articles.headline: Your Genes Have Way More Say Over How Long You Live Than Scientists Thought
articles.body: For decades, researchers figured your lifestyle was the main driver of how long you'd live, with genes playing maybe a 20% role at best. A new study from the Weizmann Institute of Science, published in the journal Science, flips that on its head. By analyzing massive twin datasets and using mathematical models to strip out deaths caused by accidents and external factors, researchers found that genetics may actually account for roughly half of the differences in how long people live. That's double or more the previous estimate. The good news: the other half is still up to you. Lifestyle choices can shift your genetic baseline by about five years in either direction.
articles.takeaway: Your genes set a ceiling, but healthy habits can still add meaningful years. Focus on the things you can control: exercise, diet, and sleep.

articles.topic: menopause brain grey matter memory loss
articles.headline: Menopause Is Changing Women's Brains. Here's What a 125,000-Person Study Found.
articles.body: A major new study from the University of Cambridge analyzed brain scan data from nearly 125,000 women and found something significant: postmenopausal women showed measurable reductions in grey matter volume in the hippocampus, entorhinal cortex, and anterior cingulate cortex. Those are the areas responsible for memory, emotional regulation, and focus. These same regions are often affected early in Alzheimer's disease. Women not on hormone replacement therapy also showed slower reaction times. Researchers say menopause may accelerate brain aging in ways that matter long term. The practical message: a healthy lifestyle during perimenopause and beyond is not optional.
articles.takeaway: Exercise, quality sleep, and a strong diet are especially important for women in their 40s and 50s to help protect brain health through and after menopause.

articles.topic: ashwagandha cortisol balance women clinical trial
articles.headline: Ashwagandha Doesn't Just Lower Cortisol. It Balances It. That Difference Matters.
articles.body: A new clinical study focused on women aged 30 to 59 found that a standardized ashwagandha extract called Shoden did something more nuanced than simply suppressing cortisol. It balanced it, keeping levels in a functional range throughout the day rather than flattening the curve entirely. The study measured cortisol levels, sleep quality, stress, mood, and cognitive function including brain fog. Shoden also stayed active in the body for up to 24 hours, significantly longer than most ashwagandha extracts. Researchers noted promising results in mood, focus, and mental clarity beyond the stress-reduction benefits.
articles.takeaway: If you're using or considering ashwagandha, look for a standardized extract. Balancing cortisol is the goal, not eliminating it.

articles.topic: stress heart disease brain nervous system link
articles.headline: Stress Isn't Just Ruining Your Mood. It's Rewiring Your Heart Risk.
articles.body: Research from Mass General Brigham helps explain exactly why chronic stress is so dangerous for your heart. The study points to three key mechanisms: heightened stress-related activity in the amygdala, a dysregulated autonomic nervous system, and chronic inflammation. Together, these pathways translate psychological pressure into measurable cardiovascular damage. Notably, people who met physical activity recommendations had lower stress-related brain activity and a 23% lower risk of heart disease. For people with depression, exercise was roughly twice as effective at reducing heart risk. Even 20 minutes of daily movement made a difference.
articles.takeaway: Exercise is your most powerful tool against stress-driven heart risk. Aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate movement per week and two strength sessions.

quote.text: The mind is the athlete; the body is simply the means it uses to run faster or longer, jump higher, or perform better.
quote.author: Bryce Courtenay
quote.context: Australian bestselling author known for stories about endurance, resilience, and the human will to overcome.

workout.title: Steady State Long Run
workout.warmup: Start with 5 minutes of easy walking, then spend 5 minutes at a very easy jog to get the legs moving. Focus on relaxed breathing and loose arms before settling into your long run pace.

exercises.name: Easy Aerobic Running (Zone 2)
exercises.notes: Hold a comfortable conversational pace throughout. You should be able to speak in full sentences. Heart rate should stay in Zone 2, roughly 60-70% of max. Do not push the pace.

exercises.name: Final Mile Negative Split
exercises.notes: Finish the last mile slightly faster than your average pace. Nothing aggressive, just a gentle pickup to practice finishing strong on tired legs.

workout.cooldown: Walk for 5 minutes after you finish to bring your heart rate down gradually. Follow with light standing calf stretches and a quad stretch on each leg, holding 30 seconds each.

product.name: Brooks Glycerin 22
product.blurb: The gold standard daily trainer. Soft enough for easy days, structured enough for long runs.

meal.title: Garlic Butter Shrimp and White Bean Skillet
ingredients: 1 lb large raw shrimp, peeled and deveined
1 can (15 oz) white cannellini beans, drained and rinsed
Salt and black pepper to taste
meal.instructions: Heat olive oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat. Season shrimp with paprika, salt, and pepper, then cook 2 minutes per side until pink. Remove and set aside. Reduce heat to medium, add butter and garlic, and cook 60 seconds until fragrant. Add broth and white beans, simmer 3-4 minutes until slightly reduced. Stir in spinach until wilted, return shrimp to the pan, squeeze lemon juice over everything, and toss to combine. Serve immediately.
meal.tip: Frozen shrimp works perfectly here. Thaw it fast by running cold water over it in a colander for about 5 minutes. Pat dry before cooking so you get a proper sear instead of steaming.

product.name: Skratch Labs Hydration Mix
product.blurb: Clean hydration without artificial junk. What Chris uses on long training days.
